skill.name: wordpress-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized WordPress security assessments using WPScan, manual enumeration, vulnerability review, credential testing, and exploitation workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized WordPress security assessments with clear scope and risk controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives in context: the skill contains executable examples for network enumeration, credential attacks, Metasploit modules, reverse shells, and webshell upload. The material is dual-use and includes authorization language, but the operational exploitation and evasion guidance make it high risk and not safe for general marketplace publication without restriction.

high_findings.title: Credential Brute Force Workflows
high_findings.description: The skill provides WPScan commands for WordPress password attacks against single and multiple users, XML-RPC brute force, high thread counts, throttling, and targeted wordlist creation. This is directly usable for credential compromise if run outside an authorized test.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly use WPScan password attack modes with user and password lists. The surrounding text labels the workflow as brute-force credential testing.

high_findings.title: Metasploit Exploitation and Shell Upload
high_findings.description: The skill requires Metasploit and includes modules for WordPress admin shell upload and plugin exploitation, with target, credential, listener, and exploit steps. These examples move beyond assessment into interactive compromise.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Metasploit is named as a required tool and concrete exploit modules are configured step by step. The intent is confirmed by the section title and exploit commands.

high_findings.title: Reverse Shell and Webshell Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill shows a PHP reverse shell using exec and a plugin webshell that runs commands from a request parameter. It also describes uploading and accessing the malicious plugin, which creates a direct remote command execution path.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The PHP examples directly invoke operating system commands and expose command execution through a web request. The text calls the plugin malicious and gives the access URL.

high_findings.title: Detection Evasion and Proxy Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes Tor and HTTP proxy configuration, random user agents, throttling, proxy rotation, and advice to avoid aggressive modes on monitored sites. This can help attackers reduce detection during scanning and brute force activity.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Proxy and user-agent controls can be legitimate in authorized testing, but the text explicitly frames them as detection evasion. That context raises confidence in the risk assessment.

medium_findings.title: Network Enumeration and Vulnerability Scanning Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many curl, Nmap, and WPScan examples that enumerate WordPress paths, users, themes, plugins, backups, and database exports. These commands are expected for a penetration-testing skill but can support unauthorized reconnaissance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly network-active reconnaissance commands against a target host. They are common in legitimate assessments, so severity is medium when considered alone.

medium_findings.title: XML-RPC Attack Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill explains XML-RPC method listing and multicall brute-force request construction. This can be used to bypass login protections and scale credential guessing attempts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly describes XML-RPC brute force and says it may bypass protection. The included request body demonstrates repeated credential attempts.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs and Local Proxy Addresses
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and loopback IP addresses are examples such as target.com and 127.0.0.1, not evidence of data exfiltration or hidden command-and-control infrastructure. They remain network-active examples if copied by a user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are common placeholders and local proxy addresses in visible documentation. No external collection endpoint or secret transfer is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Mostly Path Text
low_findings.description: Several weak-cryptography detections appear to come from WordPress path names, XML-RPC text, or command examples rather than actual cryptographic implementation. No evidence found that the skill implements or recommends weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown guidance, not executable crypto code. The cited regions contain paths, shell examples, and tables rather than algorithm selection logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct PHP Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: A PHP example calls exec with a shell command that opens a reverse shell to an operator-controlled host.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly executes a bash reverse shell. There is no benign interpretation for this pattern outside controlled exploitation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Request-Driven Webshell
dangerous_patterns.description: A plugin example checks a request parameter and passes it to system, allowing remote command execution through an HTTP request.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command parameter flows directly into system and is then accessed through a URL. This is a textbook webshell pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Password Attack Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: WPScan commands use username and password inputs for brute-force attacks against WordPress login and XML-RPC endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section is titled Password Attacks and the commands use explicit password attack options. The risk is confirmed by both syntax and context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Exploit Framework Modules
dangerous_patterns.description: Metasploit modules are selected and configured for WordPress shell upload and plugin exploitation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use exploit modules and call exploit after setting target and credential parameters. This is active exploitation guidance.

content.value_statement: WordPress assessments require structured discovery, enumeration, and reporting. This skill organizes authorized testing workflows for WordPress sites and highlights operational risks.
actual_capabilities: Structures WordPress discovery checks for common paths, metadata, feeds, and login endpoints.
Explains WPScan usage for version, theme, plugin, user, backup, and database export enumeration.
Covers vulnerability review using WPScan API data and exploit search workflows.
Describes credential assessment methods, including login and XML-RPC password testing.
Provides report-oriented deliverables for enumeration, vulnerability, credential, and exploitation evidence.
Lists constraints such as authorization, scope, WAF blocking, rate limits, and false negatives.
limitations: The skill contains offensive examples and should only be used in authorized environments.
It does not verify legal scope or ownership of a target system.
WPScan results depend on signatures, API access, and target visibility.
Some guidance can trigger security controls or disrupt production sites.

use_cases.title: Pre-Assessment Scoping
use_cases.description: Build a WordPress testing checklist that maps discovery, enumeration, and reporting tasks to an approved scope.

use_cases.title: Internal WordPress Review
use_cases.description: Review owned WordPress installations for exposed paths, vulnerable plugins, weak configuration, and outdated components.

use_cases.title: Training Lab Walkthrough
use_cases.description: Teach WordPress assessment phases in an isolated lab where exploitation and credential testing are explicitly permitted.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Safe Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized WordPress security assessment plan for my owned site. Include scope, setup, discovery steps, and reporting sections.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret WPScan Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me interpret WPScan findings from an authorized scan. Prioritize version, plugin, theme, and user enumeration risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Credential Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a low-impact credential assessment for an internal WordPress test. Include rate limits, lockout safeguards, and success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Final Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my authorized WordPress assessment notes into an executive summary, technical findings, evidence list, and remediation roadmap.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized WordPress assessment for a small business site.
output_examples.output: A phased test plan with scope confirmation, discovery checks, WPScan enumeration, manual verification, risk ranking, and remediation-focused deliverables.

output_examples.input: Summarize risks from outdated plugins and exposed XML-RPC.
output_examples.output: A concise finding summary that explains business impact, affected components, verification evidence, and recommended fixes such as updates and access controls.

output_examples.input: Prepare a report outline for a WordPress penetration test.
output_examples.output: A report structure covering engagement scope, methodology, confirmed findings, credential testing boundaries, screenshots, remediation priorities, and retest steps.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, target ownership, and testing windows before any scan or exploit attempt.
Use rate limits, backups, and monitoring contacts when testing production WordPress sites.
Convert tool output into verified findings with impact, evidence, and practical remediation.
anti_patterns: Do not scan or attack WordPress sites without explicit written permission.
Do not run brute-force, exploit, shell, or webshell examples on production systems without approved scope.
Do not treat automated WPScan output as confirmed risk without manual validation.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general use?
faq.answer: It is high risk because it includes offensive WordPress testing workflows. Use it only for authorized security work.

faq.question: Does it require WPScan?
faq.answer: Many workflows use WPScan, but the skill also includes manual checks with curl, browser paths, and supporting tools.

faq.question: Can it verify legal permission?
faq.answer: No. Users must confirm authorization, scope, and rules of engagement before using any testing steps.

faq.question: Does it exploit vulnerabilities automatically?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, not executable automation. It includes examples that could guide exploitation if followed.

faq.question: What should I do with WPScan findings?
faq.answer: Validate each finding manually, map it to business impact, and provide clear remediation steps.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with strict authorization boundaries.
